speaker: interviewer
text: 1996. The survivor is Benjamin Bender, and this is tape number five. You were telling us about the black soldiers who had just come through the gates to liberate you at Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. After the liberation, I stayed in Buchenwald for about four or five weeks. Being an orphan, they were looking probably for-- for a place to place me. I wanted badly to be adopted. My family-- I wanted to be, again, a member of-- of humanity. I wanted to have brothers, sisters, close family. I felt like being a stray dog. I did look for a-- for a master by loving people, which I didn't find even after I went from-- from Germany-- I went to France, Metz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's say-- after five, six weeks, I went to Metz. In Metz, I was in a like, little camp for orphans. I met one Jewish family, living in Metz on Rue Pasteur 21, and I have hopes that maybe they will invite me to stay then, but probably no one was looking for problems at that time. I went to Palestine with orphans, with a group of orphans, and I went to a cousin, who gave me a short stay. He was not too happy about staying with them for too long. And he said, the best place for you is to stay in a kibbutz, which I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to-- to a kibbutz. I met Sara, my present wife, and in 1948, I volunteered. The war broke out. The Arab countries invaded Israel. I volunteered into the elite force of-- of Palmach, which calls for Plugot Mahatz, the crack troops. It was a unit of about 2,000 of us all over the country fighting on all the fronts. And I was in hot spots. I almost was gone, but probably providence was over me. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after the war, I was in Seville. I was a working executive for-- for-- for a big-- an enterprise. I had an exhibition in 1960-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] being a catalyst, hoping that maybe I could study commercial art. In September 21, 1962, I went to United States, America. I was studying commercial art for a short time. I had a family of two, then got four. My son was 11. I couldn't support them. I was earning $1.15 per hour, and this was another disaster. But I studied. I did it for one year and half. I couldn't support my family. I went into a business. I was successful. I brought up-- I bought up a bankrupt operation, and I built up a very progressive and modern operation. And I was enjoying. I retired at the age of 57, early, turning to-- to art, to travel, traveling all over the world, trying to recoup the losses of our life, because my youth was gone, was robbed from me. For the five years spent under the German was a carte blanche-- was a carte blanche written by my oppressors, not by myself. So I tried to create something, which I will do by myself, with my talent, my-- my ability. In-- in 1985, I wrote a letter to the refuting their report by a reporter who was in Buchenwald and stated that Buchenwald was liberated by the Russian, which was a lie. The Russian was handed to the-- the-- the American handed the Buchenwald to Russia in July of 1945, so this was out of question. After I wrote this article-- three years after that, three years after that, three years-- I got a call from PBS, Channel 13, WNET. And Bill Moyers and Nina Rosenblum, two producers, were working on a film, For three years, I was involved in filming, helping them with co-producing the film and-- and having an active part in this film.

speaker: interviewer
text: The film was nominated for Oscar, and then a dispute erupted by units of different-- from different patrols-- 10th Armored Division, 7th, the first-- who are the first? We are the first. You are-- I was the first. And the whole discussion was really useless. It was nonsense, because Buchenwald wasn't liberated by-- by force. Buchenwald was liberated because the German escaped. And the people who wanted to claim victory, did it and destroyed in a way that they didn't get the Oscar.

speaker: interviewee
text: To this very day, I am stating, again, I was interviewed on TV by many stations, 12, here in Florida, in New York, in Washington. The liberation of Buchenwald was done by American forces, by the Third Army. The patrol who entered the gate, I saw them with my eyes, to the best of my ability. I was in good shape, my mind vivid, and there is nothing to add to it. And the black soldiers deserved the credit for the liberation-- not for the liberation of the camp-- for being the one who entered the gate. Maybe I was-- maybe there was other guys who saw different people, but I saw the patrol, black patrol, on April 11, 1945. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When you look back upon your experiences in the Holocaust, why do you think that you survived? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I stopped to believing in miracles. I ceased to believe in divine help. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was a willpower, and there was a dream. And I think that-- that life without a dream is a nightmare. You have to have a dream, even a small dream. In our life, you aspire for professional. We aspire to establish a family life, or establish one personal goal. It has to be dream because there are disappointments. There's a lot of frustration, a lot of rejection. My book was rejected many, many times. I didn't give up. I knew that I have a duty, a moral duty to put this in writing, and people should know, not only in America, all over the world. And I did it, in one way. My was viewed by 12 million people in Europe, United States, in Australia, in Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I think it has a lot to do with your stamina, with your-- with your inside power, with your soul, because there is no-- there is-- there is no other way. You have to look inside yourself, and this-- and this what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you often think about the Shoah?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you often think about the Shoah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too much now as I used to do before. I think [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with the film and with my book and with my exposure in the United States, I'm coming to terms with myself. I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there is no hatred. There is pain because the wounds-- the wounds never turn to scars. The sorrow had never a chance to recover. This is something which is done, and this I have to carry through the rest of my days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to make this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think I owe this to, not only to the living and to the dead, to their memory, but to the posterity-- to the generation to come, especially now in times when people refuse to believe that Holocaust took place, that the numbers are exaggerated, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that places like Treblinka, Auschwitz, Buchenwald, Dachau, Bergen-Belsen, Mauthausen, are fiction. And there is a-- a danger, because people tend to forget. People have to be reminded on a daily basis. If this is pleasant or not, no one should care about this. Sometimes if you take medicine, if it is bitter, we swallow, because you want to-- to live. If they don't believe, let him swallow the bitter pill, let him know what happened. I'm just afraid that the passing of the generation-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's so close, it's very close. And you cannot burden our grandchildren and our children to carry the blazing stories of Holocaust. No, they cannot do it. They cannot relive our pain again and again. They have to live a normal life. They have to know what happened to their parents, and we-- we have to close the chapter. And to leave this for other generations, they should analyze, they should study, and they should read the conclusion of the truth, record the truth, only the truth.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your most painful memory of the Shoah?

speaker: interviewee
text: The mostly painful memory, I think, there are two, two memories-- the death of my parents [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and the feeling of being rejected, which I wasn't. They found an exit with honor. They couldn't share. They didn't want to share their death with me, and they didn't do it. They didn't tell me, because I wouldn't leave them.

speaker: interviewer
text: The second thing is my brother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's more than anything else. I loved him very much. He was gentle. He was-- he was noble, and he was a pious Jew. There was a feeling of guilt, and still is for many, many years and years to come-- why me and not him? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is shadowing my life. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Do you have a last message, or a message that you would like to leave us with?

speaker: interviewee
text: A message to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Message-- do you have a message that you would like to leave for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I have a message. I have a message. There is a lot of hatred, a lot-- a lot of ignorance, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not among morons only, but among scholars-- people pretending to be illuminated by wisdom, by knowledge. They are ignorant, though, and this is the biggest danger. We have to fight bigotry. We have to fight arrogance, human arrogance. We have to fight superiority. We are born equal, under one God. Forget the religion-- Christians, Jews, Muslims, [INAUDIBLE]-- we are people. Our blood is red-- one color. Our skin is a rainbow of colors, but it does mean nothing. We can be good friend with black, with Chinese. It is a dangerous trend sweeping over the United States. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't fear any Holocaust, because we are living in a democracy, in a beautiful land what should be cherished by everyone. Everyone has a share in the happiness because life is short. We are only guests on this planet. We are only one-- only the same fingerprints, only one-- never to be duplicated. The moment we reach understanding, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that we should respect each other. Not-- we don't have to love each other. We don't have to do it, but we should respect each other. Religions should respect religion. Muslims should respect Jews, and Jews should respect Muslims, and Christians and Jews, and vice versa. It's a moment of religious kind of understanding between people that we are being born equal. Babies are the same. There was no different between German babies and Jewish babies, no difference. They have blue eyes. They have blond-- blond hair. They have the same brilliant, beautiful smile, but there was a difference, because of hatred, because of arrogance, because of blind obedience, because of bigotry and a complex of superiority. No one is above the other guy. We are all equal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for your sharing with you my memories, and thank you for this historical foundation, for giving me this splendid opportunity to share with you my memories. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please tell me who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Blima Bender, maiden name Szylit-- S-Z-Y-L-I-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in Czestochowa around 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Would you please tell me who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our wedding picture-- myself, Sara, and it was taken in Israel in Kiryat Motzkin, October 14, 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Would you please tell me who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our daughter, Ayelet, and our son-in-law, [? Gaetano. ?] This picture was taken in New York. This is their wedding picture, and they live now in Berkeley, Oakland, California.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken about, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I would say, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] six years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's about 1990.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Would you please tell me who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is as our son, Avi and our daughter-in-law, [? Lili. ?] The picture was taken about three years ago. They live in Potomac, Maryland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Would you please tell me who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is our grandchild, a grandson, Michael. He will be bar mitzvah-- he was bar mitzvahed last year, and Rachael, our granddaughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken? This taken-- was taken in-- in Israel. And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Last year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Would you please introduce this lovely lady that's sitting next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sara Bender, my lovely wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mrs. Bender, would you like to just tell us a few things about your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I know my husband for almost 50 years, since we were teenagers. And we're not only husband and wife, we are the best of friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful. Is there anything, Mr. Bender, that you would like to add now that you might have forgotten to mention before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Before we went into the ghetto, we are still living at the [INAUDIBLE] Street. I walk in the street, not wearing an armband, because we have to use these. At a certain part of the street, I was encountered by two Hitler-Jugend. They were in the Hitler-Jugend uniform, carrying long knives. And my friend, a Polish friend, pointed at me-- 'a Jew.' And they approached me in a very dangerous way, because to get me up or to do whatever. I recall there were two tall, tall, tall guys. I recall I punched one in his face. I knock him down, and he just rolled down on the street. This points to my-- how naive I was. At the time, what I did was almost insane. And I was running to my-- my house. I was an excellent runner. They didn't even try to run after me. I reached my house, breathless, and I told the story to my-- to my mother. My mother was crying. In a matter of 15 minutes, a group of about 60 or 80 Hitler-Jugend with their leader surrounded our house, looking for me. Someone pointed up, so they know exactly where I was, and probably, they knew that I was a Jew. Even being a Pole, I could face the same thing. In a matter of minutes, a German priest appeared in our backyard. I knew him, because there was a German Gothic church not far from our house. And he got involved in a very heated discussion with the leader of this group. I think it was about maybe a half an hour. After half an hour, they dispersed. They dispersed. They left the place. I recall I didn't leave my house for two weeks, and then we had to move to the ghetto. So the whole thing was-- but I think, thanks to the German priest, I owe him my life, because I would be sent to Gestapo and executed. A Jew couldn't attack a German. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to thank you very, very much for telling us your story--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for giving a testimony today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Thank you so much.
